name: Step-by-Step: Filing a Claim for Workplace Chemical Exposure
description: Step-by-Step: Filing a Claim for Workplace Chemical Exposure - Ogden

step.name: Step 1
step.text: ### How to Handle a Chemical Exposure Incident at Your Job

step.name: Step 2
step.text: Chemical exposure is different from a typical "trip and fall." The injuries are often internal, invisible, and progressive. If you have been exposed to toxic fumes, solvents, or hazardous substances, follow this step-by-step guide to protect your health and your claim.

step.name: Step 3
step.text: **Step 1: Decontaminate and Seek Air**

step.name: Step 4
step.text: Immediately move to an area with fresh air. If the chemical touched your skin or eyes, use an emergency wash station for at least 15 minutes. Do not wait until the end of your shift.

step.name: Step 5
step.text: **Step 2: Identify the Substance**

step.name: Step 6
step.text: You have a legal "Right to Know" under OSHA. Ask your supervisor for the **Safety Data Sheet (SDS)** for the chemical. This document tells your doctor exactly what toxins are in your system and what treatment is required. Take a photo of the SDS with your phone.

step.name: Step 7
step.text: **Step 3: Document Your Symptoms Immediately**

step.name: Step 8
step.text: Toxic exposure often causes "delayed onset" symptoms. Keep a daily log of:

step.name: Step 9
step.text: - Difficulty breathing or wheezing

step.name: Step 10
step.text: - Headaches and dizziness

step.name: Step 11
step.text: - Skin rashes or chemical burns

step.name: Step 12
step.text: - Nausea or metallic tastes in your mouth

step.name: Step 13
step.text: **Step 4: See a Specialist**

step.name: Step 14
step.text: General practitioners often miss the signs of toxic organ damage. Ask for a referral to an **Occupational Medicine** specialist or a toxicologist. Tell them exactly which chemicals you were exposed to and how long the exposure lasted.

step.name: Step 15
step.text: **Step 5: File the State-Specific Forms**

step.name: Step 16
step.text: - **In Utah:** Ensure your employer files Form 122.

step.name: Step 17
step.text: - **In Idaho:** File a notice of injury with the Industrial Commission.

step.name: Step 18
step.text: - **In Texas or Colorado:** Follow the specific state reporting guidelines for occupational diseases.

step.name: Step 19
step.text: ### Common Pitfalls to Avoid

step.name: Step 20
step.text: - **Do Not Quit Your Job:** Leaving voluntarily can kill your right to lost wage benefits.

step.name: Step 21
step.text: - **Avoid Recorded Statements:** The insurance company will ask "trick" questions to get you to admit you weren't wearing PPE.

step.name: Step 22
step.text: - **Don't Ignore "Minor" Fumes:** Long-term exposure to low levels of toxins can be just as deadly as a large spill.

step.name: Step 23
step.text: ### Securing Legal Support

step.name: Step 24
step.text: Chemical cases are the hardest workers' comp claims to win because insurance companies always argue the illness is "personal" or "age-related." Just Work Comp Law has the expertise to fight these denials. Jared L. Mortenson and our team understand how to use Safety Data Sheets and expert testimony to win "active" and "occupational" illness claims.

step.name: Step 25
step.text: Call 801-746-0290 to discuss your exposure case with a lawyer who cares about workers, not corporate profits.
